name: Italian Game
history: One of the oldest recorded openings, analyzed in Italy in the 1500s. It remains a favorite first opening for beginners and a serious weapon even at world-championship level.
ideas: Develop quickly and castle early
Aim the bishop at the weak f7 square
Build a strong pawn center with c3 and d4
Keep your pieces working together toward the center
plans: Slow build-up: play d3, c3, Re1, Nbd2-f1-g3 and prepare d4 later
Watch for tactics on f7 once your knight can jump to g5
Expand on the queenside with b4 and a4 in the middlegame
mistakes: Playing Ng5 too early and losing the knight for nothing
Forgetting to castle and getting attacked in the center
Trading the strong c4-bishop without a good reason

quiz.q: What weak square does the Italian bishop on c4 target?
quiz.why: Before castling, f7 is defended only by the king — the classic target of the Italian Game.

quiz.q: What is White's usual plan with the c3 pawn move?
options: To stop Bb4 checks and prepare d4
To open the c-file for the rook
quiz.why: c3 supports the d4 push, building the ideal pawn center, and takes b4 away from Black's pieces.

name: London System
history: Named after the London tournament of 1922, this solid system became hugely popular in the 2010s. You can play almost the same setup against nearly anything Black does.
ideas: Get the dark-squared bishop OUT to f4 before playing e3
Build the solid pawn triangle c3-d4-e3
Develop pieces to the same reliable squares every game
Attack the kingside once your position is safe
plans: Standard setup: Bf4, e3, Bd3, Nbd2, c3, then castle
Plant a knight on e5 and support it with f4 for a kingside attack
Meet ...c5 calmly with c3, keeping your pawn wall intact
mistakes: Playing e3 before Bf4 and locking your bishop inside
Ignoring Black's queenside play completely
Trading dark-squared bishops when it helps Black

quiz.q: Which piece must develop BEFORE you play e3 in the London?
quiz.why: If you play e3 first, your c1-bishop is locked behind its own pawns — the number one London mistake.

quiz.q: What pawn structure is the heart of the London System?
quiz.why: That solid triangle makes White's position very hard to attack.

name: Queen's Gambit
history: Documented as early as 1490 and the star of a world-famous TV series, the Queen's Gambit is the most respected way to fight for the center after 1.d4 d5.
ideas: Offer the c-pawn to pull Black's pawn away from the center
It is not a real gambit — you can usually win the pawn back
Take space and develop smoothly
Pressure the queenside with your rooks
plans: Against the accepted (…dxc4), play e4 or e3 and regain the pawn with Bxc4
In the declined, pressure d5 with Bg5, e3, and Rc1
Use the minority attack: push b4-b5 to create a weak black pawn
mistakes: Chasing the c4 pawn too hard after ...dxc4 and falling behind in development
Letting Black free the position with an easy ...c5 or ...e5
Trading your good bishop on g5 for no reason

quiz.q: Is the Queen's Gambit a true gambit (a real pawn sacrifice)?
options: No — White can almost always regain the pawn
Yes — White gives up the pawn forever
quiz.why: If Black tries to keep the c4 pawn with ...b5, tricks like a4 and Qf3 win the material back.

options: Pull Black's d5 pawn away from the center
quiz.why: If Black captures, White dominates the center with e4 next.

name: Ruy Lopez
history: Named after a Spanish priest who wrote about it in 1561. Called 'the Spanish torture' because White can squeeze Black slowly for the whole game. A favorite of nearly every world champion.
ideas: Pressure the knight that defends the central e5 pawn
Keep the tension — don't rush to capture on c6
Build a long-term space advantage
The bishop retreats to b3 and stays powerful for the whole game
plans: The classic maneuver: Nbd2-f1-g3 heading for f5
Build the big center with c3 and d4
Meet Black's ...b5 and ...Na5 by keeping your light-squared bishop safe on c2
mistakes: Capturing on c6 without a concrete reason
Allowing the Noah's Ark trap: after careless play the bishop gets trapped by ...c4
Forgetting the e4 pawn needs protection after Black plays ...b5 and ...Na5

options: Pressure the knight defending the e5 pawn
quiz.why: The bishop pressures c6 — the defender of e5 — creating long-term threats against Black's center.

quiz.q: After 3...a6, why doesn't White just take the knight?
options: Black regains the pawn easily after Bxc6 dxc6 Nxe5 with Qd4
Taking is actually the best move
quiz.why: After 4.Bxc6 dxc6 5.Nxe5 Qd4! forks the knight and e4-pawn, so White gains nothing.

name: Scotch Game
history: Made famous in a correspondence match between Edinburgh and London in 1824, and revived by Garry Kasparov in the 1990s as a surprise weapon.
ideas: Open the center immediately with d4
Get free, active piece play
Avoid heavy opening theory
Fight for the initiative from move 3
plans: Develop with Be3, c3 and Bc4 or Be2, then castle
Use your extra central space to start a kingside initiative
If Black plays ...Nf6, consider the aggressive e5 push
mistakes: Moving the d4 knight repeatedly and losing time
Neglecting the e4 pawn
Letting Black trade everything into an easy endgame

options: Opens the center before Black is developed
quiz.why: White trades off the e5 pawn immediately to free the pieces and seize space.

quiz.q: After 3...exd4 4.Nxd4 Bc5, what does the bishop attack?
quiz.why: The bishop hits d4, so White usually defends with Be3 or c3.

name: Sicilian Defense
history: The most popular and highest-scoring reply to 1.e4 at every level of chess. Named after the Italian island, it turns the game into an unbalanced fight from the very first move.
ideas: Fight for the center from the side with the c-pawn
Trade your c-pawn for White's d-pawn to get a central majority
Use the half-open c-file for your rooks and queen
Accept a sharp, double-edged battle
plans: The Najdorf setup: ...a6, ...e5, ...Be7 and quick queenside counterplay
Put pressure down the c-file with ...Rc8 and ...Qc7
Meet White's kingside attack with a queenside pawn storm — race!
mistakes: Falling behind in development while grabbing pawns
Ignoring White's f4-f5 or g4 attacking plans
Letting White land a knight on d5 permanently

quiz.q: What does Black gain by trading the c-pawn for White's d-pawn?
quiz.why: Black ends up with two central pawns versus one — a long-term structural edge.

quiz.q: Which file becomes Black's highway for counterplay?
quiz.why: After ...cxd4 the half-open c-file is where Black's rooks and queen go to work.

name: French Defense
history: Named after a Paris vs London correspondence match in 1834. A tough, strategic defense where Black accepts a cramped position in exchange for a rock-solid structure and counterattacking chances.
ideas: Build the solid e6-d5 pawn chain
Strike at White's pawn chain with ...c5 (and later ...f6)
Accept the 'bad' light-squared bishop as the price of solidity
Counterattack the d4 base of White's pawn chain
plans: Hit d4 with ...c5, ...Nc6 and ...Qb6 all together
In the Winawer, give up the bishop pair to damage White's queenside pawns
Play ...f6 at the right moment to challenge the e5 wedge
mistakes: Never solving the problem of the c8 bishop
Playing ...f6 too early and exposing the king
Allowing White a free attacking setup with Bd3 and Qg4

quiz.q: What is Black's main pawn break in the French?
quiz.why: The whole French strategy revolves around attacking White's pawn chain at its d4 base with ...c5.

quiz.q: Which Black piece is famously difficult to develop in the French?
quiz.why: The e6 pawn blocks its own bishop — finding it a future is Black's eternal French problem.

name: Caro-Kann Defense
history: Named after Horatio Caro and Marcus Kann who analyzed it in 1886. World champions Capablanca, Botvinnik and Karpov trusted it for its safety and sound structure.
ideas: Support ...d5 with the c-pawn instead of the e-pawn
Develop the light-squared bishop OUTSIDE the pawn chain first
Get a French-style structure without the bad bishop
Aim for a healthy, safe middlegame and a better endgame
plans: Classical setup: ...Bf5, ...e6, ...Nd7, ...Ngf6, ...Be7 and castle
Play ...c5 later to challenge White's center
Head for endgames where your solid pawns shine
mistakes: Leaving the bishop on f5 where h4-h5 traps it — retreat to g6 in time
Playing too passively and never striking back with ...c5
Underestimating White's kingside space after h4

quiz.q: What advantage does the Caro-Kann have over the French Defense?
options: The light-squared bishop develops outside the pawns
quiz.why: By playing ...c6 instead of ...e6 first, the c8 bishop gets to f5 before ...e6 closes the door.

quiz.q: Why does the bishop retreat from f5 to g6 after White's Ng3?
options: To avoid being trapped and stay on the strong diagonal
quiz.why: On g6 it keeps its diagonal and can recapture toward the center if White trades.

name: King's Indian Defense
history: A hypermodern favorite of attacking legends Tal, Fischer and Kasparov. Black lets White build a big center — then attacks it, and the white king, with everything.
ideas: Fianchetto the bishop to g7 — the 'dragon's eye'
Let White take the center, then strike it with ...e5 or ...c5
When the center locks, race: Black attacks the king, White attacks the queenside
The g7 bishop and the ...f5-f4 pawn storm are your weapons
plans: Classical: after d5 by White, play ...f5, ...f4 and storm the kingside with pawns
Reroute knights: ...Ne7-g6 or ...Nd7-f6 to support ...f5
Sacrifice material if it opens lines to White's king — the g7 bishop is worth a rook
mistakes: Playing ...f5 before completing development
Trading the g7 bishop lightly — it is the soul of the opening
Answering White's queenside play instead of racing on the kingside

quiz.q: What is Black's typical winning plan in the Classical King's Indian?
quiz.why: When the center is locked, Black throws the kingside pawns forward at White's king — one of the most exciting plans in chess.

quiz.q: Which piece is called 'the soul of the King's Indian'?
quiz.why: The fianchettoed bishop supports both the ...e5 strike and the kingside attack — never trade it carelessly.
